title: Introduction to Git

Structure of tutorial
Mention that all examples assumes the user is using Git for Windows?

title: Introduction to Git

Acknowledgements




Chacon, Scott, and Ben Straub. 2021. “Pro Git.” https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2.

title: Appendix A — Git BASH
text:
A.1 Shortcut keys
Git for Windows is a set of tools for Windows users. Of main interest, Git for Windows provides a Bash emulation used to run Git from the command line. Using Git Bash, Windows users can use “git” commands exactly like one would do in Linux and Unix environments. Besides the full set of Git core commands, Git Bash is also packaged with additional shell commands that can be useful.
When you open Git Bash, you will see something like this:
The user_id@computer_id is an ID based on name of the user that is logged in, and the name of the device running Git Bash. The MINGW64 part is the name of the compiler used to build Git Bash. The ~ at the end of the first line indicates the current directory. A tilde means that the current directory is the home directory of the user.
An extensive list of available commands in Bash can be found here. Not all of them are available in Git Bash. Below is a short list of useful shortcut keys.
Windows users are used to using Ctrl+cCtrl+c and Ctrl+vCtrl+v to “Copy” and “Paste”, but these shortcuts are not available in Git Bash, since they are reserved for other commands. it is possible to enable “Copy” using Ctrl+Shift+cCtrl+Shift+c and “Paste” using Ctrl+Shift+vCtrl+Shift+v, by right-clicking in the terminal, navigating to Options>Keys, and checking the “Ctrl+Shift+letter shortcuts” box.

title: Appendix A — Git BASH
text:
A.1 Useful Git BASH commands
Below is a list of useful basic shell commands.



Command
Description




cd
Change directory


clear
Clear terminal window


echo
Display a line of text


ls
List directory content


mkdir
Make directories


pwd
Print working dierctory


rm
Remove files


rmdir
Remove empty directories


touch
Change file timestamps



TODO: look into “start .” trick to open current directory
In the following we will show how these commands can be used to create, manipulate, and delete files.

A.1.1 cd


A.1.2 TODO: update old code below
To clear window use clear command. alternatively press Ctrl+lCtrl+l).
In this example we will illustrate how to use the above commands in Git Bash, by creating and manipulating files, and then deleting them again afterwards.
First, we use cd to change the directory to where we want to create a new folder.
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 ~
$ cd "C:/some/directory"
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/some/directory
$

Git for Windows also comes with shell integration, so alternatively we can simple navigate to C:/some, right click on the “directory” folder (shift+right click on Windows 11) and select “Open Git Bash here”. The current working directory can be seen




Chacon, Scott, and Ben Straub. 2021. “Pro Git.” https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2.

title: Appendix A — Git BASH
text:
A.2 Git BASH shortcut keys
talk about configuration of shortcut keys, in particular ctrl+c and ctrl+v, that on Windows is normally used for copy and paste, but is not availble in the terminal since these shortcuts are typically reserved for other shortcuts in terminals, eg ctrl+c is used to exit/interrupt a currently running process.
Suggest this change: https://superuser.com/questions/1639562/how-to-customize-shortcut-keys-on-git-bash-terminal-windows-10

title: Appendix A — Git BASH
text:
A.2 Configuaration of Git BASH
In this section we will briefly show how Git Bash can be configured.

A.2.1 Shortcut keys
Shortcut keys can be configured by right-clicking in the terminal and navigating to <span style="font-family:monospace">Options>Keys.</span>. Of main interest to Windows users, is the possibility to reconfigure the “Copy” and “Paste” shortcuts. Windows users are used to using Ctrl+cCtrl+c and Ctrl+vCtrl+v to “Copy” and “Paste”, but these shortcuts are not available in Git Bash, since they are reserved for other commands. By default the “Copy” shortcut is Ctrl+insCtrl+ins and “Paste” is Shift+insShift+ins, which some Windows users might find very unfamiliar to use. If the “Ctrl+Shift+letter shortcuts” box is ticked, it is possible to “Copy” using Ctrl+Shift+cCtrl+Shift+c and “Paste” using Ctrl+Shift+vCtrl+Shift+v which might feel more familiar.
An extensive list of available commands in Bash can be found here. It is unknown if all of them is available in Git Bash. Below is a short list of useful shortcut keys we will comment on.



Description
Shortcut
Alternative shortcut




Clear the screen, similar to the clear command
Ctrl+l




Make a list of useful shortcut keys. eg ctrl+c kills process? shortcuts for navigating cursor in code?

title: Appendix A — Git BASH
text:
A.2 Configuration of Git BASH
In this section we will briefly show how Git Bash can be configured.

A.2.1 Shortcut keys
Shortcut keys can be configured by right-clicking in the terminal and navigating to Options>Keys. Of main interest to Windows users, is the possibility to reconfigure the “Copy” and “Paste” shortcuts. Windows users are used to using Ctrl+cCtrl+c and Ctrl+vCtrl+v to “Copy” and “Paste”, but these shortcuts are not available in Git Bash, since they are reserved for other commands. By default the “Copy” shortcut is Ctrl+insCtrl+ins and “Paste” is Shift+insShift+ins, which some Windows users might find very unfamiliar to use. If the “Ctrl+Shift+letter shortcuts” box is ticked, it is possible to “Copy” using Ctrl+Shift+cCtrl+Shift+c and “Paste” using Ctrl+Shift+vCtrl+Shift+v which might feel more familiar.
An extensive list of available commands in Bash can be found here. Not all of them are available in Git Bash. Below is a short list of useful shortcut keys.



Description
Shortcut




Moving the cursor



Go to beginning of line (Home)
Ctrl+a


Go to end of line (End)
Ctrl+e


Previous command (Up arrow)
Ctrl+p


Next command (Down arrow)
Ctrl+n


Go left/back one word
Alt+b


Go right/forward one word
Alt+f


Go forward one character
Ctrl+f


Go backward one character
Ctrl+b


Editing



Copy
Ctrl+ins


Paste
Shift+ins


Clear the screen
Ctrl+l


Delete the character before the cursor (Backspace)
Ctrl+h


Delete the character after the cursor
Ctrl+d


Process control



Interrupt/kill current process (SIGINT)
Ctrl+c

title: Appendix A — Git BASH
text:
A.2 Git BASH commands
It is unfortunately not straightforward to get an overview of all available commands in Git Bash. The full list of available commands can be assessed from files in the /usr/bin directory of the Git Bash installation. Documentation of Linux shell commands in general, and how to use them, can be found with some google-fu. For example, here.
Here we give a (not necessarily detailed) description of some of the available shell commands in Git Bash.





Command
Description




cd
Change working directory


clear
Clear terminal window


echo
Display a line of text


ls
List directory content


mkdir
Make directories


pwd
Print working dierectory


rm
Remove files


rmdir
Remove empty directories


start .
Open current directory in explorer


touch
Change file timestamps






A.2.1 cd
Syntax: cd [dir] Changes the current directory to dir.
The default dir value (~) is the value of the $HOME shell variable, the home directory of the user.
Note that forward slashes (/) are used to specify file paths, and that paths can be both on the form C:/path/to/directory and /c/path/to/directory
If the file path contains spaces, enclose the file path in (double) quotes.
Git for Windows also comes with shell integration, so an alternative to using cd we can change the working directory by navigating to the folder in Windows explorer, right click on the folder (shift+right click on Windows 11) and select “Open Git Bash here”.

A.2.1.1 Example - Change directory
We can use cd to change the working directory to a specific folder by providing a path to the folder
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 ~
$ cd C:/path/to/directory
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/path/to/directory
$

Note that the working directory was set as C:/path/to/directory, but is displayed on the form /c/path/to/directory


A.2.1.2 Example - file path with spaces
If a file path contains spaces, enclose the file path in (double) quotes
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 ~
$ cd "/c/path/to/some directory"
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/path/to/some directory
$



A.2.1.3 Example - move to sub directory
To change the current working directory to a subfolder of the current working directory, we can simple set dir to the subfolder name
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/path/to/dir
$ cd subfolder"
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/path/to/dir/subfolder
$



A.2.1.4 Example - move to parent directory
We can move the working directory to the parent directory by setting dir to “..”
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/path/to/dir
$ cd ..
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/path/to
$

You can also use “..” more generally when specifying a file path, to denote the parent directory
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/path/to/dir_a
$ cd ../dir_b
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/path/to/dir_b
$



A.2.1.5 Example - Move to previous directory
We can change the working directory to the previous working directory by setting dir to “-” ( “-” is converted to $OLDPWD).
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/current/cd/dir
$ cd -
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/previous/cd/dir
$



A.2.1.6 Example - Move working directory to user’s home directory
As mentioned earlier, “~” denotes the user’s home directory. We can set current working directory by setting dir to “~”, or simply by not specifying it at all.
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/current/dir
$ cd ~
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 ~
$ cd -
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/current/dir
$ cd
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 ~




A.2.2 clear
Syntax: clear Clear the terminal screen.

A.2.2.1 Example
Imagine we had the history shown in the terminal below, ending with a clear command.
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 ~
$ cd C:/path/to/directory
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/path/to/directory
$ clear

This would result in the terminal shown below.
user_id@computer_id MINGW64 /c/path/to/directory
$




A.2.3 echo


A.2.4 ls
Syntax: ls [OPTIONS]... [FILE]...
List information about the FILE’s (in the current directory by default). Sorts entries alphabetically by default.
Curated options -a, –all Do not ignore entries starting with dot (.), i.e. hidden files. -1 List one file per line -R, –recursive List subdirectories recursively.


A.2.5 mkdir


A.2.6 pwd


A.2.7 rm


A.2.8 rmdir


A.2.9 start .
TODO: probably change to just “start” also in table, then explain how we can ALSO use it to open current directory in explorer


A.2.10 touch
.




Chacon, Scott, and Ben Straub. 2021. “Pro Git.” https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2.
